2004-02-01.title: Doctor in Italy Tries to Ease Pain of an African Tradition
2004-02-01.abstract: Dr Omar Abdulcadir of Florence, Italy, treats African victims of female genital cutting; has asked local health officials for permission to perform less severe and largely symbolic operation under hospital conditions to lessen pain and risks of infection; issue is part of larger debate on rights of Europe's immigrant community to continue traditional practices; those who cling to tradition either take their daughters to Africa for procedure or have it done illegally in Europe; photo (M)

2004-02-01.title: Pakistan Ousts Nuclear Scientist From Post
2004-02-01.abstract: Pakistani government removes scientist Abdul Qadeer Khan, founder of country's nuclear weapons program, from post as special adviser to prime minister; Khan and other scientists and military officers are implicated in illicit sale of nuclear technology to Iran, Libya and other countries; it is not clear whether Khan or others will be prosecuted; Pakistani Pres Pervez Musharraf has been under competing pressures from United States, secular and Islamic political rivals and politically powerful army in pursuing investigation; photo (M)

2004-02-01.title: Powell's Case, a Year Later: Gaps in Picture of Iraq Arms
2004-02-01.abstract: Secretary of State Colin L Powell's warnings to United Nations Security Council one year ago about Iraq's banned weapons programs have not been borne out by findings of Iraq Survey Group; his carefully worded presentation was considered Bush administration's most comprehensive public case about urgent threat Iraq posed to world; his reputation for caution and care gave it added credibility; now Dr David A Kay, Washington's chief weapons inspector as head of Iraq Survey Group, says Iraq had no significant stockpiles of chemical or biological weapons when invasion began in March; Washington is seized by question of how and why such an intelligence gap happened; interviews with intelligence officials, Iraqi engineers and others suggest that Powell's case was largely based on limited, fragmentary and mostly circumstantial evidence, with conclusions drawn on basis of little challenged assumption that Saddam Hussein would never dismantle old illicit weapons and would pursue new ones to fullest extent possible; photos; excerpts from Powell's Feb 2003 speech at UN and from Kay's recent testimony and interviews; drawings; map (L)

2004-02-01.title: Yukihiko Ikeda, 66, Japanese Lawmaker
2004-02-01.abstract: Yukihiko Ikeda, a longtime political figure in Japan, led his countrys efforts to resolve the 1990s hostage crisis in Peru.

2004-02-01.title: 3 G.I.'s Among 12 Killed in Bombings in Iraq
2004-02-01.abstract: Twelve people die in two bomb attacks in northern Iraq; suicide bomber attacks police station in Mosul, killing at least nine people and wounding 45; three American soldiers are killed when bomb destroys their Humvee in Kirkuk; Iraqi officials fear there could be more violence during visit next week by UN team to assess feasibility of holding elections in June; photo (M)

2004-02-02.title: 56 KURDS KILLED IN SUICIDE BLASTS IN NORTH OF IRAQ
2004-02-02.abstract: Two suicide bombers kill at least 56 people and wound at least 200, Erbil, during Muslim holiday fetes at separate headquarters of Iraq's two leading Kurdish political parties; blasts shatter calm of north, part of Iraq that had been relatively stable under American occupation; blasts, 10 minutes apart, kill several top leaders and wound other senior officials; come as two parties have been trying to unite region's divided administrations to strengthen demands to retain area's autonomy; American military officials, while expecting increase of violence during holiday, say there was little preparation to guard against suicide bombers; some officials suspect foreign fighters or Ansar al-Islam, mostly Kurdish terrorist group that may have ties to Al Qaeda, were involved in bombing; photos (M)

2004-02-02.title: Key Pakistani Is Said to Admit Atom Transfers
2004-02-02.abstract: Dr Abdul Qadeer Khan, founder of Pakistan's nuclear arms program, reportedly has confessed that during last 15 years he has provided Iran, North Korea and Libya with designs and technology to produce fuel for nuclear weapons; senior Pakistani officials give Pakistani journalist exhaustive and startling account of how Khan, national hero, spread secret technology; say he presided over network that smuggled hardware, shared secret designs and gave personal briefings to Iranian, Libyan and North Korean scientists in covert meetings abroad in what he called effort to ease Western attention on Pakistan and help Muslim cause; carefully avoid pinning any blame on Pres Pervez Musharraf, army or Pakistani intelligence service, despite fact that some of material apparently was transported on government cargo planes; say all transfers ceased in early 2002; United States believes transfers to Libya continued through fall of 2003; it is unclear whether Khan, whose house has been surrounded by security officials for weeks, will be arrested; photo (M)

2004-02-02.title: Hague Journal; Another Serb Defendant Stays on His Best Bad Behavior
2004-02-02.abstract: Serbian ultranationalist and warlord Vojislav Seselj, known for his rabble-rousing speeches and foul language, has brought those habits to Hague, where he faces charges involving war crimes in Bosnia and Croatia in 1990s; sneers at United Nations court as American tool against Serbs and demands judges be disqualified; some court officials wonder how such an obstructionist defendant can get proper and fair trial; he is conducting own defense, which allows him to hold floor in court, even now at preparatory hearings; photo (M)

2004-02-02.title: BUSH TO ESTABLISH PANEL TO EXAMINE U.S. INTELLIGENCE
2004-02-02.abstract: Pres Bush plans to set up bipartisan panel to examine American intelligence operations, including study of possible misjudgment about Iraq's unconventional weapons; panel will also probe failures to penetrate secretive governments and stateless groups that could attempt new attacks on United States; decision follows rising pressure from both Democrats and many ranking Republicans to deal with errors that overstated Iraq's stockpiles of chemical and biological weapons and its progress toward developing nuclear arms; administration officials describe plans for broader examination of intelligence shortcomings to retool intelligence-gathering for new era of terrorism and nuclear proliferation by rouge scientists and nations that may provide arms for groups like Al Qaeda; panel will not report back until well after Nov elections (M)

2004-02-02.title: An Inquiry That's Awash In Disputes at the Outset
2004-02-02.abstract: Intelligence officials have reluctantly begun to admit that major overhaul could be in order in wake of their failure to anticipate Sept 11 terrorist attacks and misjudgment of Iraq's arms stockpiles; hope independent panel Pres Bush will appoint can offer them more help and less finger pointing; have often derided recommendations of such panels as naive or unworkable; are least upset by Rep Porter J Goss's stand that any new inquiry should look forward, rather than dwell on past mistakes; Sen John D Rockefeller IV and others want inquiry to take Bush administration to task for building what they call flawed case that United States needed to act quickly against Iraq (M)

2004-02-03.title: Chirac Backs Longtime Ally Now Guilty Of Corruption
2004-02-03.abstract: French Pres Jacques Chirac passionately defends Alain Juppe, his former prime minister and longtime political partner, who was convicted in corruption scandal dating from days when Chirac was Paris Mayor; others in Chirac's cabinent, including Prime Min Jean-Pierre Raffarin and Min Dominique de Villepin, earlier lauded Juppe; Magistrates' Union scores comments as threat to separation of powers; government probes obstruction of justice charges in case brought by Judge Catherine Pierce; photo (M)

2004-02-03.title: Angering Settlers, Sharon Says Most May Have to Leave Gaza
2004-02-03.abstract: Prime Min Ariel Sharon, outraging members of settlement movement he helped create, says he might seek to evacuate almost all Israeli settlers from Gaza Strip, newspaper interview; move is part of 'unilateral disengagement' from Palestinians he plans should Bush administration's peace plan fail; says he ordered plans for evacuation of 17 of at least 20 settlements; his aide says option is most far-reaching of three he will submit to cabinet; opponents on right and skeptics on left charge he seeks to divert attention from bribery scandal; settlers warn of political action to bring down his government; far-right parties do not immediately bolt from his governing coalition; Palestinians suspect public relations ploy; photos (M)

2004-02-03.title: Wolfowitz Gets an Earful on Ethnic Tensions
2004-02-03.abstract: Deputy Sec Paul D Wolfowitz, on mission to highlight successes in Iraq, attends Kirkuk town council meeting and hears of ethnic and religious tensions that are vastly complicating American efforts for orderly turnover of sovereignty; holds all Iraq was Saddam Hussein's victim and that all Iraqis have equal stake in cooperating to build new and democratic nation; Maj Gen Raymond T Odierno addresses objections of Shiites and other groups to caucus system planned by United States to select initial new government (M)

2004-02-03.title: World Briefing: Asia
2004-02-03.abstract: NEPAL: Protesters Injured; VIETNAM: Pirates Attack;.

2004-02-03.title: World Briefing | Americas: Ecuador: Indian Leader Attacked
2004-02-03.abstract: National Indian Federation leader Patricia Gualinga reports failure of assassination attempt on federation head Leonidas Iza; Iza is unharmed, but three relatives, including his son, are injured; group opposes free-market reforms proposed by President Lucio Gutierrez; photo (S)

2004-02-04.title: World Briefing | United Nations: Palestinian Criticizes Israeli Envoy
2004-02-04.abstract: Palestinian observer mission head Nasser al-Kidwa criticizes Israeli ambassador to United Nations Dan Gillerman for his recent comments faulting UN Sec Gen Kofi Annan's response to Jerusalem suicide bombing (S)

2004-02-04.title: Blair Fields Criticism, and Heckling, as He Defends War
2004-02-04.abstract: Prime Minister Tony Blair said today that freedom and democracy in Iraq were more important than any mistaken intelligence.

2004-02-04.title: Rumsfeld Defends U.S. Intelligence Operations
2004-02-04.abstract: Defense Secretary Donald H. Rumsfeld told a Senate committee that weapons of mass destruction might yet be found in Iraq.

2004-02-04.title: Bush Presses U.N. to Mediate Iraqi Clash on Rule
2004-02-04.abstract: Pres Bush presses United Nations Sec Gen Kofi Annan at White House meeting to have his aides mediate among quarreling factions in Iraq and forge consensus behind plan that would allow transfer of sovereignty to Iraqi government by June 30; Bush aides say Bush, without rapid progress on political issues, might agree to delay self-rule as last resort; Bush offers Annan dozen transfer options, ranging from holding direct elections before June 30 to radically overhauling unwieldy caucus system that is supposed to choose new national assembly; Annan, after meeting, says UN team heading soon to Iraq to assess possibility of direct elections will expand its agenda; says UN has chance to help break impasse; photo (M)

2004-02-04.title: Debate Begins In France On Religion In the Schools
2004-02-04.abstract: Prime Min Jean-Pierre Raffarin opens debate in French legislature on bill banning religious symbols in public schools; holds Muslim head scarves must be banned because they undermine republican ideal of freedom and equality; calls on Muslims to behave like good citizens; bill has divided cabinet and enraged Muslim minority, while heartening both Muslim and non-Muslim feminist groups; photo (M)

2004-02-05.title: U.N. Leader to Hold Talking on Reuniting Cyprus
2004-02-05.abstract: Secretary General Kofi Annan has invited Greek Cypriot and Turkish Cypriot leaders to New York next Tuesday.

2004-02-05.title: Russia Considers Extending Presidential Term to 7 Years
2004-02-05.abstract: As written, bill could allow President Putin to run for two new terms, conceivably keeping him in power until 2018.

2004-02-05.title: Iran's Supreme Leader Tries to Defuse Crisis
2004-02-05.abstract: Iran's supreme religious leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei seeks to resolve confrontation between reformist supporters of Pres Mohammad Khatami's government and their hard-line opponents; orders review of disqualifications of thousands of parliamentary candidates (S)

2004-02-05.title: World Briefing | Europe: Spain: Outcry As Bishops Tie Battering To Sex
2004-02-05.abstract: Treatise on family presented by Spanish Roman Catholic Bishops Conference causes uproar from women's rights groups and politicians; holds domestic violence is negative consequence of sexual liberation for women (S)

2004-02-05.title: Tory Leader Calls on Blair to Quit for Iraqi Weapons Claim
2004-02-05.abstract: The leader of the Conservative Party of Britain, Michael Howard, called on Prime Minister Tony Blair today to resign.

2004-02-06.title: Sharon May Shift Gaza Strip Settlers to the West Bank
2004-02-06.abstract: The prime minister envisions a swap of sorts — giving up most of the Gaza Strip, but holding on to large chunks of the West Bank.

2004-02-06.title: World Briefing | Europe: Spain: Catalonia Region Pushes Gay Rights
2004-02-06.abstract: Catalonian government will allow civil union for same-sex couples and allow them to adopt children; other regions are awaiting Constitutional Court ruling on legality of gay marriagein Spain (S)

2004-02-06.title: At Least 39 Killed in Bomb Blast in Moscow Subway
2004-02-06.abstract: Russian officials said the bombing appeared to be the latest and one of the worst terrorist attacks linked to the war in Chechnya.

2004-02-06.title: World Briefing | Americas: Canada: Court Upholds Teacher's Suspension For Anti-Gay Writings
2004-02-06.abstract: British Columbia (Canada) Supreme Court rules that one-month suspension given Chris Kempling, high school guidance counselor in Quesnel who wrote series of anti-gay letters published in local newspaper, was fair and did not infringe on his freedom (S)

2004-02-06.title: Faulting U.S., Germany Frees A 9/11 Suspect
2004-02-06.abstract: German court acquits man accused of providing support to suicide pilots in 9/11 attacks, citing refusal by United States to allow testimony from suspected member of Al Qaeda in its custody; Abdelghani Mzoudi, former roommate in Hamburg of Mohamed Atta, is second suspect to be tried for involvement in attacks; German prosecutors already convicted another former roommate of Mzoudi, Mounir el-Motassadeq, on same charges: accessory to murder and membership in Al Qaeda, and based on virtually same evidence; that conviction is being appealed; prosecutors blame Mzoudi's acquittal on Bush administration's reluctance to make captured terrorists available for testimony and to allow prosecutors to make use of intelligence information on terrorist network; that stance could also imperil criminal prosecution of Zacarias Moussaoui, only person facing trial in US on charges of involvement in 9/11 attacks; at issue in both trials is testimony of Ramzi bin al-Shibh, captured Qaeda suspect who is believed to have been member of Hamburg cell and central to 9/11 plot; photo (M)

2004-02-07.title: Agency Alert About Iraqi Not Heeded, Officials Say
2004-02-07.abstract: Senior US government officials say Iraqi military defector identified as unreliable by Defense Intelligence Agency provided some of information that went into intelligence estimates that Iraq had stockpiles of biological weapons at time of US invasion; say classified 'fabrication notification' about defector, former Iraqi major, was issued by DIA to other US intelligence agencies in May 2002, but it was repeatedly overlooked (M)

2004-02-07.title: At U.S. Urging, NATO Acts to Bolster Afghan Rebuilding Role
2004-02-07.abstract: NATO defense ministers, at urging of US, agree to take specific steps to strengthen alliance's reconstruction role in Afghanistan with eye toward eventually taking over most of security and rebuilding tasks in country; Britain, Italy, Turkey and Norway will lead four of five new NATO teams of soldiers and civilians that will fan out beyond Kabul and assist local authorities with security and rebuilding (M)

2004-02-07.title: 39 DIE IN MOSCOW AS BOMB GOES OFF ON SUBWAY TRAIN
2004-02-07.abstract: Bomb explodes inside Moscow subway train during morning rush on Feb 6, killing at least 39 people and wounding over 130; no one claims responsibility for bombing though senior government officials, including Pres Vladimir V Putin, suspect that it is latest in series of terrorist attacks linked to war in Chechnya; hundreds of passengers have to stagger through smoke-filled tunnels to reach safety; Putin blames wave of terror on separatist leader Aslan Maskhadov; map; photos (M)

2004-02-07.title: Assassinations Tear Into Iraq's Educated Class
2004-02-07.abstract: Hundreds of intellectuals and mid-level administrators have been assassinated since May in widening campaign against Iraq's professional class; Brig Gen Mark Kimmitt says by silencing urban professionals, guerrillas are waging war on Iraq's fledgling institutions and progress itself; dead include doctors, lawyers and judges; US authorities say foreign terrorists may be behind attacks; Iraqi authorities point to former Baath Party elements or displaced military officers; say killings have been coordinated; photo of Khalid Ali al-Mayah, whose brother Prof Abdul al-Latif al-Mayah was recently assassinated (M)

2004-02-07.title: U.S. Calls Report of Bid to Kill Sistani False
2004-02-07.abstract: Senior US military official says people close to Grand Ayatollah Ali al-Sistani, Iraq's most powerful spiritual leader, maintain that accounts of assassination attempt against him on Feb 5 were 'fabrication'; contradictory reports about possible attack on cleric in Najaf continue to circulate throughout country; photo (M)

2004-02-08.title: Official Afghan Report Says 10 Civilians Died in U.S. Raid
2004-02-08.abstract: Afghan government team sent to investigate Jan 17 airstrike in Uruzgan province concludes that at least ten civilians were killed; United States military denies any civilian died in raid; maintains AC-130 gunship fired on and killed five armed militants (S)

2004-02-08.title: Delicate Dance For Musharraf In Nuclear Case
2004-02-08.abstract: Some former and current American officials say there was considerable evidence that Gen Pervez Musharraf turned blind eye to Dr Abdul Qadeer Khan's selling of nuclear secrets to Iran, North Korea and Libya, even as Musharraf says he lacked proof; with recent confession by Khan and pardon, Musharraf continues balancing act, privately calling Khan 'bad news,' but praising him publicly and giving him title 'special adviser'; US has been engaged in its own balancing act, weighing Pakistan's lack of action against Khan with its need to support Musharraf government in campaign against terrorism; details leading to confrontation between Musharraf and Khan and Khan's apology to nation described; Western experts say Khan, who is seen in Pakistan as founder of nuclear program, is egomaniac who is expert at making centrifuges but is not due all credit he is receiving; deal struck by Khan leaves many questions unanswered, including whether government officials or other scientists kenw about or played role in nuclear network; since Musharraf's pardon, US has refrained from criticism or any further action against Pakistan, but Simon Henderson, Washington Institute for Near East Policy, says there is much more potentially embarrassing information that could come to light (M)

2004-02-08.title: Unemployed Iraqis Say New Jobs Will Pay Off in Greater Security for All
2004-02-08.abstract: Economic recovery and security are inextricably linked in Iraq; recent poll of Iraqis in six cities found 98 percent say job creation would be 'very effective' in improving security, but investment that could produce new jobs is not likely amid current violence and chaos, and unemployment rate is estimated at 22 percent to 30 percent; United States will direct half of $18.7 billion reconstruction funding to local employers rather than foreign contractors, creating up to one million jobs over next few years; photo (M)

2004-02-08.title: Ride During Moscow Rush Ends Under Pile of Bodies
2004-02-08.abstract: Bomb explodes on Moscow subway train during morning rush hour, killing at least 39 people and wounding more than 130; investigators suspect Chechen separatists are behind attack (M)

2004-02-08.title: A Mother's Ordeal Forces Britain to Review Crib Deaths
2004-02-08.abstract: British appeals court overturns Angela Cannings's conviction and life sentence for smothering two of her children, finding that conviction rested on presumption of guilt rather than evidence; Cannings lost three of her four children to sudden infant death syndrome; case calls into question so-called Meadow's law, which says one such death is tragedy, two are suspicious, and third is murder 'unless proven otherwise'; attorney general, Lord Goldsmith, announces review of 258 similar criminal cases and may review civil family court cases as well; photo (M)

2004-02-09.title: U.S. Says Files Seek Qaeda Aid In Iraq Conflict
2004-02-09.abstract: Letter seized in raid on known Al Qaeda safe house in Baghdad contains detailed proposal for waging 'sectarian war' in Iraq in next few months; Americans say letter was written by Abu Musab al-Zarqawi, Jordanian identified by Bush administration in days before war as main link between Al Qaeda and Saddam Hussein's government; letter notes that extremists are failing to enlist support inside country, and have been unable to scare Americans into leaving; proposes mounting attacks on Iraq's Shiite majority with aim of prompting counter attacks against Arab Sunni minority; says such a 'sectarian war' will rally Sunni Arabs to religious extremists; says war against Shiites must start soon, before Americans hand over sovereignty to Iraqis; American officials in Baghdad say that they are confident letter is credible and that they have independently corroborated Zarqawi's authorship; document, if authentic, constitutes strongest evidence to date of contacts between extremists in Iraq and Al Qaeda; but it does not speak to debate about whether there was Qaeda presence in Iraq during Saddam Hussein era, nor is there any mention of collaboration with Hussein loyalists; photo (M)

2004-02-09.title: U.S. and Australians Reach Wide-Ranging Trade Accord
2004-02-09.abstract: United States completes negotiations for free trade agreement with Australia; proponents call it landmark deal that could increase American manufacturing exports by as much as $2 billion annually; agreement is rare trade victory for Bush administration, reached as America's annual trade deficit and loss of manufacturing jobs to foreign competition are festering as issues in presidential election year; photo (M)

2004-02-09.title: The Debate over Responsibility for the War
2004-02-09.abstract: George W. Bush is on the defensive. His CIA director confirms the absence of weapons stockpiles in Iraq and the national budget is moving toward a record deficit. With the election nine months away, the opposing Democratic camp is bursting with confidence.

2004-02-09.title: U.S.-Russian Plan to Destroy Atom-Arms Plutonium Is Delayed
2004-02-09.abstract: Project to destroy plutonium from thousands of retired Russian and American nuclear weapons is delayed, prompting fears of some experts that work may never be done; immediate reason for delay is that United States and Russia are deadlocked on liability rules for American workers and contractors that would help build factories in Russia that could mix uranium with plutonium, to be burned as fuel for civilan reactors (M)

2004-02-09.title: Libya's Crude Bomb Design Eases Western Experts' Fear
2004-02-09.abstract: Investigators determine that nuclear weapon blueprints found in Libya from Pakistani scientist Abdul Qadeer Khan were of his own relatively crude type of bomb--not more advanced models that Pakistan developed and successfully tested; analysis of blueprints has heartened investigators in Europe and United States because his design is seen as less threatening in terms of spread of nuclear weapons (M)

2004-02-10.title: World Briefing | Africa: South Africa: Minister Defends Garlic Aids Diet
2004-02-10.abstract: South African Health Minister Manto Tshabalala-Msimang repeats her advice for boosting immune system, which she believes will keep HIV positive individuals from developing AIDS; suggested diet of garlic, olive oil and lemon juice was denounced by leading national medical journal last year; at least one in 10 South Africans is estimated to be HIV positive; photo (S)

2004-02-10.title: Blair to Meet Qaddafi, After Libya Aide Has Talks in London
2004-02-10.abstract: Prime Minister Tony Blair will meet the Libyan leader, Col. Muammar el-Qaddafi, "as soon as convenient," a British official said.

2004-02-10.title: U.S. Aides Report Evidence Tying Al Qaeda to Attacks
2004-02-10.abstract: Senior American officials think that Abu Musab al-Zarqawi, Jordanian suspected of ties to Al Qaeda, probably played role in at least three major car-bomb attacks in Iraq that have killed over 100 people in last six months: bombing of Shiite mosque in Najaf and United Nations headquarters in Baghdad, and of Italian police headquarters; say document seized in raid on safe house in Baghdad, believed to have been written by Zarqawi, indicated that he had directed about 25 suicide bombings inside Iraq; say that items seized in raid, in addition to document, included seven-pound block of cyanide salt, which could have spread cyanide gas within enclosed area; Hassan Ghul, one of Zarqawi's top lieutenants, was arrested by Americans near Iranian border last month and has been interrogated by American military and intelligence officials (M)

2004-02-10.title: Ban on Religious Apparel Advances in France
2004-02-10.abstract: Frances National Assembly voted today to ban Muslim headscarves and other religious symbols from public schools.

2004-02-10.title: Jordanian's Role In Iraq Bombings
2004-02-10.abstract: A Jordanian suspected of ties to Al Qaeda, Abu Musab al-Zarqawi, is now thought likely to have played a role in at least three major car bombing attacks in Iraq that have killed more than 100 people in the last six months, senior American officials said.

2004-02-11.title: Freed Afghan, 15, Recalls a Year at Guantánamo
2004-02-11.abstract: Muhammad Ismail Agha, 15, one of three young Afghans recently released from Guantanamo Bay, comments on his 14-month imprisonment as terrorist suspect, interview, Nawzad, Afghanistan; is bitter about being held for so long for no wrongdoing; expresses anger at Afghan militia that arrested him, not at Americans; photo (M)

2004-02-11.title: Inching to Reconciliation, Blair Plans Meeting with Qaddafi
2004-02-11.abstract: British Sec Jack Straw, after he and Prime Min Tony Blair meet with Libyan Min Muhammad Abdul Rahman Shalghma, London, say Blair will meet Col Muammar el-Qaddafi 'as soon as convenient;' says talks focused on 'good progress' made since Qaddafi said he was ridding nation of nuclear, chemical and biological arms programs; Shalghma says Libya had equipment, material and know-how but decided not to produce unconventional arms; says he hopes families of victims of Libyan-sponsored terrorism understand that it is in everyone's interest to restore ties with Libya (M)

2004-02-11.title: Anti-Semitism infuses debate over head scarves
2004-02-11.abstract: [Library note: due to strikes, the third edition dated February 11th 2004 was not printed. ]

2004-02-11.title: Up to 80 Killed In Bomb Blasts At 2 Iraqi Sites
2004-02-11.abstract: Up to 80 people are killed and at least 76 hurt in two Iraqi car bomb blasts almost 24 hours apart; most of casualties are Iraqis applying for jobs with new Iraqi Army and police forces; latest blast occurs outside Iraqi Army recruiting station, killing at least 25 Iraqis; station is next door to volunteer center of prominent Shiite political party; earlier bombing, Iskandariya, ripped through police station where many Iraqis had lined up to apply for jobs; latest attack comes after American officials release details of intercepted document supposedly written by suspected Jordanian terrorist, Abu Musab al-Zarqawi, in which he boasts of directing some 25 suicide bombings in Iraq and asks Al Qaeda help in igniting sectarian war; photos (M)

2004-02-11.title: U.S. and Allies in New Effort to Get Along
2004-02-11.abstract: Bush administration says allies are expressing new willingness to work with United States to combat spread of nuclear and other illicit weapons around world, in part to constrain Bush from acting unilaterally, despite blow to American credibility stemming from failure to find such weapons in Iraq; admit significant damage to reputation of American intelligence will make it harder in future to rally support for confrontations over banned arms; Hans Blix, former United Nations arms inspector in Iraq, who opposed invasion, says war has convinced world of American and British seriousness and of need to work with International Atomic Energy Agency and UN (M)

2004-02-12.title: French schools fight deeply rooted racism : Anti-Semitism infuses scarf debate
2004-02-12.abstract: Head scarves were banned by administrators at the public school in this Paris suburb long before France began its current round of tortured debate on the issue.

2004-02-12.title: U.S. General Escapes Injury in Iraq After Attack by Rebels
2004-02-12.abstract: It was not immediately clear if Gen. John Abizaid, commander of all American forces in the Middle East, was the target of the attack.

2004-02-12.title: Citing Rocket Fire, Israeli Army Raids Gaza, Killing 15
2004-02-12.abstract: Israeli Army raid in Gaza City and second raid in southern Gaza Strip ignite some of fiercest fighting in months, leaving 15 Palestinians dead and about 50 injured; Israel says raids are response to sustained Palestinian rocket and mortar fire from Gaza; Prime Min Ariel Sharon said last week that he is considering plans to withdraw most of 7,500 Israeli settlers from Gaza Strip; Palestinians and Israelis had predicted that Israel would step up operations in Gaza before any Israeli withdrawal, so that pullout would not be interpreted as sign of weakness; photos (M)

2004-02-12.title: U.S. Officials Hint at Support for Haitian Leader's Ouster
2004-02-12.abstract: Senior State Dept official says Bush administration favors dialogue to ease Haiti's crisis, but that it might support replacing Pres Jean-Bertrand Aristide, who has two years left in his term; administration places itself in unusual position of saying it may accept ouster of democratic government as Haitian crisis deepens, with violence flaring and Aristide locked in impasse with his opponents; administration officials stop short of calling for Aristide's resignation, but their remarks are seen as emboldening widening and unwieldy opposition that seeks his removal (M)

2004-02-12.title: World Briefing | Europe: France: News Director Out Over Erroneous Report
2004-02-12.abstract: Oliver Mazerolle, news director of state-owned France 2, television network, steps down after vote of no confidence by journalists; network recently reported inaccurate story about former Prime Minister Alain Juppe's alleged departure from politics; photo (S)

2004-02-13.title: New Targets: Attackers Shift Their Sights to the Iraqis
2004-02-13.abstract: Analysis: fourth deadly suicide bombing in Iraq in less than two weeks suggests that insurgency that has bedeviled Americans and Iraqis since collapse of Saddam Hussein's government is changing in important ways; attacks against American soldiers have dropped since November, as insurgents increasingly focus on Iraqi civilians, particularly those who are seen to be collaborating with American-led occupation; suicide bombings have aimed to inflict maximum damage on Iraqi institutions like police and military that are central to American effort to turn over reins of government by June 30; some American and Iraqi officials call these changes evidence that insurgency is being sustained by foreign fighters with links to international terrorist groups like Al Qaeda, as ranks of Hussein's cadre are thinned by capture or death; photo (M)

2004-02-13.title: Greek and Turkish Cypriot Leaders Agree on End to Split
2004-02-13.abstract: The leaders agreed to a plan by the U.N.s secretary general intended to end the 30-year division of the Mediterranean island.

2004-02-13.title: House Panel Visits Libya to Check U.S. Intelligence
2004-02-13.abstract: Six members of the House Intelligence Committee are scheduled to meet in Libya on Friday with Col. Muammar el-Qaddafi and other top Libyan officials, a senior lawmaker said on Thursday.     The meeting with Colonel Qaddafi will be the second between an American Congressional delegation and the Libyan leader since Libya agreed to work with British and American officials to dismantle its once-secret chemical and biological weapons program. The members of the House panel are hoping to use that opening to gauge the accuracy of earlier American intelligence about Libya.

2004-02-13.title: Former Chechen Leader Killed in Car Explosion in Qatar
2004-02-13.abstract: Zelimkhan Yandarbiyev, an Islamic extremist linked to Chechen separatists, was killed today when his car exploded.

2004-02-13.title: U.N. Envoy, Visiting Iraq, Backs Cleric On Elections
2004-02-13.abstract: United Nations envoy Lakhdar Brahimi, in Baghdad, says he supports Grand Ayatollah Ali Al-Sistani's call for elections to install new sovereign government in Iraq; does not say whether he thinks elections could be held by May 31, date by which Bush administration hopes to put transitional national assembly in place to appoint new government; UN spokesman says UN team, which is lead by Brahimi, found consensus among Iraqi leaders that elections are best way to establish 'fully representative and legitimate government'; Bush administration has yet to acknowledge that security poses problem for elections, but many experts say country is too unstable to stage fully democratic process; photo (M)

2004-02-14.title: Cyprus Greeks and Turks Agree On Plan to End 40-Year Conflict
2004-02-14.abstract: Greek and Turkish Cypriot leaders accept Sec Gen Kofi Annan's plan for ending decades-long division of Cyprus and pledge to negotiate reunification in time for island's May 1 entry into European Union; deal between Greek Cypriot Pres Tassos Papadopoulos and Turkish Cypriot leader Rauf Denktash is reached after three days of talks at United Nations; only Greek Cypriot government would be entitled to join union if reunification was not achieved; Turkey accepts plan, aware that failure in Cyprus would jeopardize its own candidacy to become first Muslim nation to join union; chronology of political events in Cyprus; map (M)

2004-02-14.title: Killing of a U.N. Observer in Congo Heightens a Mission's Fears
2004-02-14.abstract: Death of UN military observer Peter Wachaye in ambush is eerie replay of troubles that beset peacekeeping mission in Congo just under year ago; killing has sent chill through UN mission and raised new questions about adequacy of 5,000 peacekeepers stationed in Ituri Province, eastern swath of Congo thick with gold--and rival gunmen willing to fight for it (M)

2004-02-14.title: U.N. Warns Against a Hasty Vote, but Iraqis Address the Issue
2004-02-14.abstract: United Nations special envoy Lakhdar Brahimi says Iraq faces significant chance of civil strife over unresolved issue of holding elections before June 30 transfer of sovereignty; Shiite and Sunni Iraqis representing some of intellectual and tribal elite meet with Grand Ayatollah Ali al-Sistani in attempt to thwart sectarian strife (M)

2004-02-14.title: Chaos and War Leave Iraq's Hospitals in Ruins
2004-02-14.abstract: Iraqi doctors say their profession is in chaos; say hospitals were in bleak shape before US-led invasion; say international isolation and sanctions imposed after Iraq's invasion of Kuwait in 1990 shattered public health care system that was once jewel of Middle East; say war has pushed it closer to disaster; say looted supplies have not been replaced, causing severe shortages; occupation authorities say improvements are coming; photos (M)

2004-02-14.title: South Korea Sending 3,000 Troops to Iraq
2004-02-14.abstract: South Korea approves plan to send 3,000 troops to Iraq, ending months of debate over country's role there (S)

2004-02-15.title: Algeria Shows Willingness to Abandon Violent Past
2004-02-15.abstract: The relative calm at the moment in Algeria and a recent stream of visits by world leaders, suggest that Algeria may be returning to the fold of diplomacy and international commerce.

2004-02-15.title: H.I.V. Surge Catches Tradition-Bound Estonia Off Guard
2004-02-15.abstract: Estonia is ill-equipped to deal with surge in HIV infections; nearly 4,000 of 1.4 million people are known to carry virus as of 2003; epidemic that began among intravenous drug-users is moving from depressed northern areas to capital Tallinn; $11 million grant from Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria will help national health institute establish AIDS-prevention programs; photo (M)

2004-02-15.title: 4 Afghan Mine-Removal Workers Shot Dead
2004-02-15.abstract: Four Afghan mine-removal workers are killed by suspected Taliban or al-Qaeda attackers in southern Farar province; attack is latest in series in which at least 20 foreign and Afghan aid workers have been killed since March 2003 in apparent campaign to scare away foreign aid and undermine government (M)

2004-02-15.title: Bank for Human Stem Cells Starts Ethics Debate in Spain
2004-02-15.abstract: Spanish government files suit in Constitutional Court contending it has regulatory control over research at stem-cell bank in Granada opened by Andalusian government; central government says it will not block research, and plans own 'transplant and regenerative' center in Madrid, but Andalusian officials say suit is political ploy to block project and appease religious voters before national elections (M) stem-cells are typically obtained from surplus embryos from in vitro fertilizat ion, but bank plans to work with adult stem-cells until matter is resolved (M)

2004-02-15.title: An Interview With Pakistan President Pervez Musharraf
2004-02-15.abstract: Reporters from the New York Times South Asia Bureau spoke with President Pervez Musharraf of Pakistan about his vision for the country.

2004-02-16.title: Chaos Becomes a Way of Life in a Rebel-Held Haitian City
2004-02-16.abstract: Fear and chaos have become way of life in Gonaives, Haiti, key crossroads held by armed rebels calling for Pres Jean-Bertrand Aristide's ouster; possibility that rebels, led by Butteur Metayer, are backed by sinister figures from Haiti's violent past, coupled with government's weak and disorganized security arrangements, could take conflict, now limited to uprisings by small armed groups in cities across nation, to new level; Gonaives rebels apparently have not massed enough militants to take on police and pro-Aristide militants in other major cities; Aristide does not seem to have enough manpower to take back Gonaives by force; map; photo (M)

2004-02-16.title: Toll at 25 In Collapse Of Park Roof, Russians Say
2004-02-16.abstract: Death toll from roof collapse at indoor water park in Moscow is now at least 25; rescuers claw through wreckage; photos; popular tropical oasis called Transvaal Park opened in 2002 (S)

2004-02-16.title: After Attacks, Iraqi Security Looks Unready
2004-02-16.abstract: Iraqi security forces are not expected to be able to guarantee safety after planned transfer of sovereignty to Iraqi government at end of June; series of bold attacks on military and police forces culminated in overrunning of police station in Falluja; photos; at least 25 people died (M)

2004-02-16.title: Her Majestys Construction Troops
2004-02-16.abstract: While bomb attacks in and around Baghdad continue to claim hundreds of human lives, the British-controlled zone in southern Iraq has remained largely unscathed by terrorist attacks.

2004-02-16.title: Of Arms and the Men
2004-02-16.abstract: The dynamics of the presidential election have been altered by the issue of military service during the Vietnam War.

2004-02-17.title: Haiti's Embattled Leader Vows to Finish Term
2004-02-17.abstract: Haitian Pres Jean-Bertrand Aristide, defiant in face of increasingly violent opposition movement, denounces it as effort to overthrow elected government, declares that only he can save country from civil war and vows to finish term that expires in Feb 2006; interview; photo (M)

2004-02-17.title: Serbs Break Parliamentary Deadlock, but Face West's Disapproval
2004-02-17.abstract: Serbia appears on verge of forming new government after weeks of political deadlock; Vojislav Kostunica, moderate nationalist leader who has been trying to form new government, may get mandate (S)

2004-02-17.title: Halliburton Stops Billing U.S. For Meals Served to Troops
2004-02-17.abstract: Halliburton Co moves to defuse growing election-year issue by suspending billing of Pentagon for cost of feeding US troops in Iraq and Kuwait, pending resolution of dispute over number of meals served; Kellogg Brown & Root subsidiary president Randy Harl sees no admission of wrongdoing (S)

2004-02-17.title: Shareholders In Yukos Oil May Yield To Kremlin
2004-02-17.abstract: Major shareholders in Russian oil giant Yukos offer to cede control of company in exchange for release of former chief executive Mikhail B Khodorkovsky, who was arrested last October on charges including tax evasion and fraud; prosecutors dismiss offer, which also calls for freeing Platon Lebedev and Aleksei Pichugin of Yukos (M)

2004-02-17.title: World Briefing | Americas: Canada: Premier Anxious To Testify
2004-02-17.abstract: Canadian Prime Minister Paul Martin, in response to eroding public opinion for governing Liberal Party, says he is looking forward to testifying before judicial inquiry into Quebec public relations financing scandal (S)

2004-02-18.title: World Briefing | Europe: Spain: Police And Strikers Clash
2004-02-18.abstract: Seville (Spain) police clash with striking ship builders; 38 workers and 12 police are injured (S)

2004-02-18.title: Israelis Approve $22 Million For Settlements on West Bank
2004-02-18.abstract: Israeli lawmakers approve more than $20 million in new spending on Jewish settlements, mostly on West Bank; Palestinians see evidence that Prime Min Ariel Sharon remains committed to settlements, despite talk of unilateral withdrawal, particulary from Gaza (S)

2004-02-18.title: Shiite Vote Plan Would Exclude 'Sunni Triangle'
2004-02-18.abstract: Shiite leaders are pushing new plan for transfer of power in Iraq that calls for partial elections, with balloting in relatively secure Shiite and Kurdish areas but not in violence-wracked 'Sunni triangle'; proposal, growing out of emerging Shiite-Kurdish political alliance, comes as UN prepares announcement on feasibility of elections; US fears partial voting would further alienate Sunnis; Shiite political leader Mowaffak al-Rubaie, Kurdish party leader Jalal Talabani and others comment; photo; Adnan Pachachi, Sunni member of Governing Council, asks how Iraq could be sovereign without voting in center of country (M)

2004-02-18.title: World Briefing | Asia: Sri Lanka: Premier Reacts
2004-02-18.abstract: Sri Lankan Prime Minister Ranil Wickremesinghe, in broadcast on several private television channels, accuses President Chandrika Kumaratunga of jeopardizing Tamil rebel cease-fire and economic recovery; speech is first one by Wickremesinghe since Kumaratunga dissolved Parliament (S)

2004-02-18.title: Nobel Laureate In Iran Joins Boycott Of Elections
2004-02-18.abstract: Shirin Ebadi, Nobel Peace laureate, joins Iranian protesters in announcing that she will not vote in parliamentary elections in which more than 2,000 candidates were disqualified by government (S)

2004-02-19.title: U.S. Presidential Politics And Self-Rule for Iraqis
2004-02-19.abstract: Concern is growing that Pres Bush, anxious to take credit in his re-election bid for establishing self-rule in Iraq, may install Iraqi government June 30 that lacks strength to withstand pressures it faces; diplomats and even some in administration question refusal to consider delay (M)

2004-02-19.title: Jewish Leaders Appeal to E.U. to Stamp Out Anti-Semitism
2004-02-19.abstract: The Europe Union today struggled to find common ground with Jewish leaders over the definition and extent of anti-Semitism in Europe.

2004-02-19.title: U.S. to Release 5 British Citizens Held at Guantánamo Bay
2004-02-19.abstract: Foreign Secretary Jack Straw said the five would face questioning under Britains Terrorism Act 2000 upon their return.

2004-02-19.title: Names of the Dead
2004-02-19.abstract: List of American service members newly confirmed dead in Iraq

2004-02-19.title: World Briefing | Europe: Spain: E.T.A. Calls a Cease-Fire For Catalonia
2004-02-19.abstract: ETA, Basque separatist group, broadcasts video announcing cease-fire with Catalonia region; ETA leaders recently met with Catalan government; political parties throughout Spain refuse to meet with guerrilla group; photo (S)

2004-02-20.title: World Briefing | Europe: France: Toxins Treaty To Be Law
2004-02-20.abstract: France ratifies United Nations treaty banning use of dozen chemicals found to be environmetally harmful; becomes 50th nation to ratify agreement (S)

2004-02-20.title: Pending Vote, Some Iraqis See Larger Council
2004-02-20.abstract: Several Iraqi leaders say they want Iraqi Governing Council to remain in place after US transfers power back to people on June 30; reach consensus that council should be expanded to as many as 125 members from current 25 and kept in power until United Nations-assisted elections can be held in early 2005; say Bush administration's plan to replace council on June 30 with new Iraqi government selected by nationwide caucuses is too cumbersome and too foreign a concept to work in Iraq; L Paul Bremer III, top American administrator, says there are now 'dozens' of possibilities of how to transfer authority and that only fixed point is June 30 deadline; says he will not make any decisions until UN issues its recommendations, which may not be for another week, past Feb 28 deadline for interim constitution; Moktada ala-Sadr, fiery Shiite cleric, says Iraqis may rebel if constitution does not enshrine Islamic law, something Bremer has said he would veto; photo; time line for transfer of power (M)

2004-02-20.title: South Africa Is Criticized For Delay in AIDS Treatment
2004-02-20.abstract: South African government is criticized for not moving swiftly enough on its promise of last November to provide free antiretroviral medication to AIDS victims; has only recently begun to solicit proposal from pharmaceutical companies that supply drugs, pushing back treatment for thousands of patients; critics say delays are symptomatic of lack of political will to confront AIDS in South Africa, which has one of biggest AIDS epidemics in world, with estimated five million people infected with HIV, 12 percent of population (M)

2004-02-20.title: World Briefing | Africa: Swaziland: Disaster Declared In Crushing Drought
2004-02-20.abstract: United Nations World Food Program reports state of national disaster in Swaziland after third year of severe drought; appeals to foreign donors to increase contributions to meet serious need for food in many southern African nations (S)

2004-02-20.title: Top General Says U.S. Lags In Aiding Iraqis Under Fire
2004-02-20.abstract: Gen Richard B Myers, chairman of Joint Chiefs of Staff, says deadly attack on Iraqi police station in Falluja on Feb 14 revealed problems in American military support for newly trained Iraqi security services; attack has prompted review of how US troops respond when Iraqi forces come under fire; many of Iraqi police officers and militia now operating independently or in tandem with American forces still lack basic equipment, like radios and patrol cars, and some of them have expressed fear that they will be outgunned by supporters of Saddam Hussein if American troops leave prematurely (M)

2004-02-21.title: As Police Flee, Rebels Tighten Grip in Haiti's Heartland
2004-02-21.abstract: Haitian rebel soldiers, hailed as liberators by thousands of residents, enter town of Hinche, ending week in which they wrested control of central plateau from Pres Jean-Bertrand Aristide's embattled government; map; photo (M)

2004-02-21.title: Pakistani Said To Have Given Libya Uranium
2004-02-21.abstract: Malaysian investigators report finds that network led by Pakistani nuclear scientist Abdul Qadeer Khan shipped partly enriched uranium directly to Libya aboard Pakistani airplane in 2001, providing fuel stock in addition to designs and technology to make nuclear bomb; provides evidence that businessmen and engineers from Turkey, Germany, Switzerland, Britain, Dubai and Malaysia were closely involved in Libya's clandestine nuclear program; report is based primarily on Malaysian authorities interrogation of B S A Tahir, middleman in Khan's global nuclear trading network; International Atomic Energy Agency report confirms that Libya received shipment in Feb 2001 (M)

2004-02-21.title: Iranians Go to the Polls, With Little Doubt of the Outcome
2004-02-21.abstract: Iran holds parliamentary elections with more significance being placed on voter turnout than on results, as religious conservatives are certain to take back control of Parliament; reformist movement urged Iranians to boycott election after more than 2,000 of their candidates in 290-seat body were disqualified from running by council controlled by hard-liners (M)

2004-02-21.title: World Briefing | Americas: Mexico: Deal To Tighten Border
2004-02-21.abstract: United States and Mexico sign agreement to tighten border controls and provide transportation home for illegal Mexican migrants rather than dropping them at border; Asa Hutchinson, border and transportation security under secretary, holds new controls are essential for success of President Bush's proposed legislation granting temporary legal status to some immigrants (S)

2004-02-21.title: World Briefing | Europe: Italy: Parcel Bomb Injures 3 Officers
2004-02-21.abstract: Parcel bomb, discovered in Perugia (Italy), explodes wounding three police officers investigating package; several package bombs have recently been sent to politician and journalists in Italy (S)

2004-02-22.title: Names of the Dead
2004-02-22.abstract: The Department of Defense has identified 546 American service members who have died since the start of the Iraq war. It confirmed the deaths of the following Americans on Friday:    GRAHAM, Jeffrey C., 24, 2nd Lt., Army; Elizabethtown, Ky.; Company C, First Battalion, 34th Armor, First Brigade Combat Team.

2004-02-22.title: U.S. AGENCY SEES GLOBAL NETWORK FOR BOMB MAKING
2004-02-22.abstract: Government forensic investigators are finding similarities in how bombs are being made in disparate areas, leading them to surmise there is global network for bomb making; previously undisclosed intelligence operation, Terrorist Explosive Device Analytical Center or Tedac, expands on studies of past cases, but there are still many questions about who is behind various bombings, including some major suicide attacks in Iraq; analysts believe that Al Qaeda has been weakened by campaign against terrorism and lacks central command, as well as financial and recruiting structures, but investigations suggest that network may be disseminating bomb-making skills to generation of militants who have fanned out around world; analysts do not say there is evidence of single controlling entity, but suggest there might not be many people with technical skills to build larger bombs; counterterrorism officials emphasize need to find master bomb makers responsible for most lethal bombings (M)

2004-02-22.title: Judge's Illness a New Setback to Milosevic's War Crimes Trial
2004-02-22.abstract: Richard May, British judge presiding over war crimes trial of former Serbian Pres Slobodan Milosevic, falls ill and may be unable to continue; lawyers at court fear more delays in trial, which has already been delayed by defendant's illness; prosecution is near to resting case, but may be forced to do so without Judge May present; photo (M)

2004-02-22.title: Risking Death, 2 Afghan Women Collected and Detonated U.S. Cluster Bombs in 2001
2004-02-22.abstract: To protect their poor farming village, two women cleared dozens of cluster bombs with their bare hands and detonated them.

2004-02-22.title: Long Indifferent, Japanese Are Drawn to South Korea
2004-02-22.abstract: South Korea has lifted almost all restrictions on imports of Japanese popular culture; trend dates from 2002, when Japan and South Korea were co-hosts of World Cup; interest in South Korea is growing in Japan, where people are watching South Korean TV and learning Korean language; two countries have history of mistrust as legacy of Japan's colonial rule of Korea, and success of rapprochement lies largely in ability of Koreans to separate Japan's culture from its history and policies; photo (M)

2004-02-23.title: Milosevic Judge Resigns Owing To Poor Health
2004-02-23.abstract: Richard May, British judge who has led war crimes trial of former Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic for past two years, resigns unexpectedly because of health problems; had gained wide respect for his low-key skill in coping with often unruly Milosevic; tribunal president, Theodor Meron, says United Nations Sec Gen Kofi Annan will name successor soon; legal experts say new judge coming into long trial faces daunting task (M)

2004-02-23.title: Civilian Pilot Dies in Afghan Shooting
2004-02-23.abstract: Gunman attacks helicopter preparing to take off from health clinic, Toloqan, Afghanistan, killing pilot and wounding three passengers employed by American construction company Louis Berger; attack has hallmarks of Taliban; wounded are evacuated to American air base, Bagram; quick-reaction air and ground force seals off area (M)

2004-02-23.title: Palestinian Bomber Kills 8 and Wounds 50 in Jerusalem
2004-02-23.abstract: Palestinian suicide bomber kills himself and at least eight other passengers on crowded Jerusalem bus; wounds over 50 people, including teenagers on their way to school; Al Aksa Martyrs Brigades claims responsibility; calls attack retaliation for Israeli security fence and for Feb 11 Israeli raid into Gaza Strip; Palestinian leader Yasir Arafat scores attack; Rep Jerrold Nadler, at bomb site, holds attack shows 'hypocrisy' of International Court of Justice hearings on fence; photo (M)

2004-02-23.title: Attack in Rural Zimbabwe
2004-02-23.abstract: Opposition leader Morgan Tsvangirai and wife reportedly are attacked by group of youths while traveling in rural Zimbabwe but escape unharmed (S)

2004-02-23.title: LOOT: Along the Antiquities Trail; An Illicit Journey Out of Egypt, Only a Few Questions Asked
2004-02-23.abstract: Court records and dozens of interviews and documents about funeral stele excavated in Akhmim, Egypt, in 1994 and seized by federal agents in New York two years ago provide rare, detailed account of smuggling, marketing and selling of high-end antiquities; worldwide trade in looted antiquities usually begins and ends in silences, with few questions asked; stele's progress shows how seamlessly looted objects can blend into legitimate market, whether dealers are aware of it or not; stele was key prosecution weapon in conviction of American dealer Frederick Schultz for conspiring to sell stolen artifacts, even though he never sold, or even saw, stele; stele is now in Cairo Museum, trophy of Egypt's drive to reclaim its stolen treasures; photos (L)

2004-02-24.title: Hearing on West Bank Wall Draws Demonstrators
2004-02-24.abstract: International Court in The Hague opens hearing on Israeli barrier around West Bank; Palestinian Authority charges violation of international law; Israel sees necessary bulwark against suicide bombers; burned wreckage of Jerusalem bus is flown in as backdrop and demonstrators carry photos of nearly 1,000 victims of Palestinian terrorism; photo; pro-Palestinian demonstrations are also held (S)

2004-02-24.title: Al Qaeda Leader Threatens New Attacks Against U.S.
2004-02-24.abstract: A senior leader of Al Qaeda threatened further attacks in messages on audiotapes broadcast by Arab satellite channels today.

2004-02-24.title: FASHION : The unsung masters:fabricators of fabrics
2004-02-24.abstract: LONDON—  Twice a year, the cream of the fashion industry, from Vivienne Westwood and Jean Paul Gaultier to Christian Lacroix and Donna Karan, converges on Première Vision, the Paris fabric fair. This is where next season's collections really begin. It is also where the little-known fabric designers finally have their rare day in the sun.

2004-02-24.title: World Briefing | Europe: France: Far-Right Leader Out Of Elections
2004-02-24.abstract: Jean-Marie Le Pen, National Front political leader and presidential runner up in 2002 national elections, is barred from running for election in Provence-Alps-Riviera (France) region because he could not prove residency (S)

2004-02-24.title: An Odd Trial For Treason Is Winding Up In Zimbabwe
2004-02-24.abstract: Closing arguments set in year-long treason proceedings against Zimbabwe opposition leader Morgan Tsvangirai, who is charged with plotting to assassinate Pres Robert Mugabe (M)

2004-02-25.title: FASHION : The regeneration game
2004-02-25.abstract: MILAN—  Carla Sozzani looks round her iconic store at 10 Corso Como — the art gallery, the books, the objects of desire and the courtyard where restaurant tables are screened with greenery.

2004-02-25.title: World Briefing | Europe: Europewide Ban On U.S. Poultry
2004-02-25.abstract: European Union bans importing of all live poultry and eggs from United States until March 23 because of outbreak of avian influenza in Texas (S)

2004-02-25.title: Israel and Jordan Cooperate in Desert Project
2004-02-25.abstract: Israel and Jordan, in rare instance of cooperation on major project, agree to build environmental studies center on their shared desert border; will donate total of 150 parched acres and take down small part of border fence; Bridging the Rift, private group, will develop center with Cornell and Stanford Universities; Cornell president Jeffrey S Lehman comments, interview (M)

2004-02-25.title: Number of Asylum Seekers Drops To a Six-Year Low, the U.N. Says
2004-02-25.abstract: United Nations refugee agency reports increasing intolerance and tough anti-asylum measures, particularly in Europe, helped to cut number of asylum seeks by one-fifth, to 471,000 in 2003, lowest level in six years; says almost all ot those arrived in 36 industrialized nations; High Comr Ruud Lubbers warns rich industrialized nations that continued aid is needed to prevent reversal of downward trend (M)

2004-02-25.title: World Briefing | Middle East: Israel: Restrictions For Nuclear Whistle-Blower
2004-02-25.abstract: Israel's Prime Minister Ariel Sharon's office announces that Mordechai Vanunu, who is finishing 18-year sentence for divulging details of nuclear facilities to British newspaper, will be placed under government supervision after his release from prison; contends restrictions are to preserve national security; photo (S)

2004-02-26.title: Britain, Citing Terrorist Threat, Plans to Expand Its Spy Agency
2004-02-26.abstract: British Home Sec David Blunkett announces major expansion of domestic espionage agency, MI5, and calls for strengthening of antiterrorism laws, including those allowing detention of foreigners without trial; tells Parliament of evidence that terrorist cells are active in Britain; 14 foreigners are now held under terrorism regulations that expire in 2006; they may leave at any time if they can find country willing to take them (M)

2004-02-26.title: 10 Years Later in Rwanda, The Dead Are Ever Present
2004-02-26.abstract: Church in Ntarama, Rwanda, is filled with grisly remains of victims of 1994 massacre; caretaker Emmanuel Murangira himself survived blood bath that killed tens of thousands of people seeking refuge at school; photos (M)

2004-02-26.title: Results Confirm Hard-Liner Gain in Iran
2004-02-26.abstract: Final election results announced by government confirm that Iran's religious conservatives made significant gains in parliament (S)

2004-02-26.title: World Briefing | Europe: Serbia: Law Changed To Fill Presidency
2004-02-26.abstract: Serbian Parliament, responding to invalidation of past three presidential elections because of low voter turnout, repeals law requiring 50 percent turnout (S)

2004-02-26.title: Unicef Urges Countries to Improve Care of Indigenous Children
2004-02-26.abstract: UNICEF director Carol Bellamy announces new campaign to urge countries to improve health care, education and living conditions for indigenous children; makes announcement in Spain to symbolize Europe's responsibility to peoples 'devastated by colonization,' and because Spain plays leading role in UNICEF programs in Latin America (M)

2004-02-27.title: Senate Panel Presses Bush On War's Plan
2004-02-27.abstract: Senate intelligence committee, faced with Bush administration's refusal to provide certain documents related to prewar intelligence on Iraq, votes in closed session to move toward possible subpoena; administration has so far insisted that documents are covered by executive privilege (M)

2004-02-27.title: World Briefing | Africa: Zimbabwe: Treason Trial Ends
2004-02-27.abstract: Trial of opposition leader Morgan Tsvangirai is completed in Zimbabwe and judge in case indicates ruling will take at least several months of deliberation; Tsvangirai is accused of conspiring to assassinate President Robert Mugabe (S)

2004-02-27.title: U.S. Plan for Mideast Reform Draws Ire of Arab Leaders
2004-02-27.abstract: United States proposal for economic, political and cultural change in Middle East to overcome anti-Western and anti-American sentiment draws criticism from both Arab leaders and European officials, who say Bush administratation did not consult countries it seeks to reform; Bush administration says some European officials have said that they may block inititiative if there is no progress on peace between Israelis and Palestinians (M)

2004-02-27.title: For Slovenia, New Alliances Are Raising New Issues
2004-02-27.abstract: Slovenia is enmeshed in issues of national identity as it prepares to join European Union; plan for construction of mosque in Ljubljana, which would be first for country's 47,000 Muslims, has engendered petition drive for referendum; and national Parliament is debating another possible referendum on fate of thousands of former Yugoslav citizens, mostly from Bosnia and Serbia, who failed to extend their residency rights shortly after Slovenia declared independence in 1991; photo (M)

2004-02-27.title: 2 Palestinians Are Killed and Dozens Wounded in Clashes With Israeli Troops Over Barrier
2004-02-27.abstract: Israeli security forces reportedly kill two Palestinians and wound dozens more in West Bank villages of Biddu and Beit Surik as they clash with protesters in confrontation linked to Israel's separation barrier; protests highlight hearing on barrier's legality underway at International Court of Justice at The Hague; two Palestinian gunmen kill Israeli soldier in northern Gaza Strip and are themselves killed by other soldiers; Palestinian Prime Min Ahmed Qurei demands return of millions of dollars sezied by Israel in Feb 25 raid on Palestinian banks; map; photo (M)

2004-02-28.title: World Briefing | Europe: Lithuania: Russian Diplomats Expelled
2004-02-28.abstract: Lithuanian government expels three Russian diplomats suspected of trying to gain confidential information about President Rolandas Paksas's impeachment proceedings and his alleged ties to Russian organized crime (S)

2004-02-28.title: REBELS NEARING HAITIAN CAPITAL, DEEPENING PANIC
2004-02-28.abstract: Armed rebels come within 25 miles of Port-au-Prince, Haiti; Bush administration says it is pursuing diplomatic resolution, but Pentagon is drawing up plans for possible intervention by Marines; State Dept says administration is in 'very close touch' with French government, which has called for Pres Jean-Bertrand Aristide to resign and has offered to take part in multinational effort to stabilize Haiti; looters pillage warehouses at port, and at least four people are killed in violence sweeping city; map; photos (M)

2004-02-28.title: 2 Killed as Troops Fight Protesters in Venezuela
2004-02-28.abstract: Venezuelan troops and antigovernment demonstrators clash in Caracas, leaving at least two people dead and several wounded as Pres Hugo Chavez convenes summit meeting of developing countries; confrontation comes two days before government electoral board is to rule on validity of signatures opponents collected to force referendum on recalling Chavez from office; photo (M)

2004-02-28.title: European strategies to offset a weak dollar
2004-02-28.abstract: LONDON—  The feeble dollar has forced European makers of luxury goods to go to ever greater lengths to ensure that their products do not become luxuries that Americans cannot afford, while minimizing any erosion of profits.

2004-02-28.title: Names of the Dead
2004-02-28.abstract: The Department of Defense has identified 549 American service members who have died since the start of the Iraq war. It confirmed the death of the following Americans yesterday:    LASKOWSKI, Matthew C., 32, Chief Warrant Officer, Army; Phoenix; Fourth Squadron, Third Armored Cavalry.

2004-02-29.title: Lebanon Resumes Capital Punishment
2004-02-29.abstract: Lebanon resumed capital punishment in January after informal five-year moratorium; Pres Emile Lahoud, under pressure to execute Shiite Muslim convicted of murdering eight people, chose also to execute one convict from each of nation's other major creeds, a Sunni Muslim and a Christian, both of whom had also killed more than one person; human rights advocates and clergy members led protest campaign to eliminate capital punishment, and executions took place amid unusual amount of public soul-searching; photos (M)

2004-02-29.title: Iraqi Leaders Miss Deadline For an Interim Constitution
2004-02-29.abstract: Iraqi Governing Council misses Feb 28 deadline for interim constitution but continues negotiations; eight Shiite members walked out in dispute over role of Islamic law, but have since returned to resume discussions; degree of Kurdish autonomy is another divisive issue; current draft is remarkably progressive and contains extensive bill of rights; interim constitution will serve as framework for government after return of sovereignty on June 30, until national assembly is chosen to write permanent constitution (M)

2004-02-29.title: Argentine Leader Faces Tumult From the Left
2004-02-29.abstract: President Néstor Kirchner of Argentina has begun confronting his far-left flank, represented by a dogmatic and often violent "picketers movement" that claims to speak in the name of the countrys unemployed.

2004-02-29.title: Israeli Airstrike Kills 3 Militants in Gaza
2004-02-29.abstract: Israeli airstrike kills three senior members of Islamic Jihad, Mahmoud Joudah, Ayman al-Dahduh and Amin al-Dahduh, traveling in car in Gaza City; more than a dozen bystanders were hurt (S)

2004-02-29.title: Veterans of Past Murderous Campaigns Are Leading Haiti's New Rebellion
2004-02-29.abstract: Rebellion against Haitian Pres Jean-Bertrand Aristide is led by members of Fraph, Haitian Front for Advancement and Progress, which overthrew his government in 1991; Aristide is widely regarded as having squandered chance given when United States sent troops to reinstate him; Aristide disbanded army, and police force is corrupt and unable to impose order on land of lawless gangs and drug money; former soldiers, death squads and drug gangs are all coming together to remove Aristide, promising democracy and better life for Haitians; photos (M)
